did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used computational learning (machine learning) classification to evaluate the separability of malignant and benign skin lesion classes based on Elliptic Fourier Analysis coefficients and asymmetry. It also employed computer vision techniques, specifically k-means clustering and morphological closing algorithms, for automated lesion boundary segmentation.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the analysis of a dataset of 993 clinical images of Non-Melanoma Skin Cancer (NMSC) and benign lesions. It describes specific methodologies including Elliptic Fourier Analysis (EFA), asymmetry calculation, multivariate statistics (MANOVA, PCA), and computational learning classification to evaluate lesion separability. The study reports new results, such as a balanced accuracy of 0.786 and an AUC of 0.735, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Novel Approach for the Shape Characterisation of Non-Melanoma Skin Lesions Using Elliptic Fourier Analyses and Clinical Images
brief_summary: This study investigated the utility of shape characterization for distinguishing Non-Melanoma Skin Cancer (NMSC) from benign lesions using a dataset of 993 clinical images from the Dermofit Dataset. The researchers employed Elliptic Fourier Analysis (EFA) and asymmetry calculations to quantify lesion morphology, utilizing k-means clustering and morphological closing for automated boundary segmentation. Multivariate statistics and computational learning classification were applied to evaluate the separability of malignant and benign classes. The best-performing approach, combining EFA coefficients and asymmetry, achieved a balanced accuracy of 0.786 and an Area Under the Curve of 0.735. The results demonstrated that shape and asymmetry are significant indicators of malignancy, supporting their integration into future clinical screening tools.

lesion_types_studied.details: n = 239; included in the study as a Non-Melanoma Skin Cancer (NMSC) type.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (Benign vs. Malignant skin lesions)
all_task_types.details: The study employed Support Vector Machines (SVMs) to classify skin lesions into benign or malignant categories based on Elliptic Fourier Analysis (EFA) coefficients and asymmetry indices. The primary task was binary classification, with performance evaluated using balanced accuracy (0.786) and Area Under the Curve (0.735). Additionally, multivariate statistical tests (MANOVA, Mahalanobis distance) were used to assess the separability of malignant (BCC, IEC, SCC) versus benign (BEN) classes.

datasets_used.name: Dermofit Dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of standard camera clinical images of skin lesions provided by the University of Edinburgh. The dataset includes masks delimiting lesion areas. The study utilized a subset of this dataset, reducing the original 10 classes to 4 specific classes for analysis. The scale of the images is unknown, and no dermoscopes were employed.

use.training: The dataset was used to extract lesion boundaries and calculate Elliptic Fourier Analysis (EFA) coefficients and asymmetry. These features were then used to train computational learning classifiers (Support Vector Machines) to evaluate the separability of malignant and benign classes.
use.validation: The dataset was used to perform multivariate statistical analyses (MANOVA, PCA) and to validate the performance of the classification models via confusion matrices and Area Under the Curve (AUC) metrics.
use.testing: The dataset was used to test the classification models, with specific results reported for the 'Combined EFA & Asymmetry' approach (balanced accuracy 0.786, AUC 0.735).
use.other: The dataset served as the primary source for empirical approximation of skin lesion shape characterization.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Square Root of the Biweight Midvariance (√BWMV)
Variance explained by Principal Components (PC scores)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Dimensionality reduction of Elliptic Fourier Analysis coefficients to evaluate variance representation.
Assessment of normality (homogeneity of sample distributions) to determine if parametric or robust statistical methods should be used.
Assessment of statistical differences and similarities among groups (malignant vs. benign lesions).
Used for MANOVA when PC scores fit a Gaussian distribution.
Robust alternative to Hotelling–Lawley for MANOVA when normality was rejected.
Comparison of within-group covariance distributions with distances to members of other groups to assess distribution differences.
Statistical assessment of distribution differences for Gaussian data.
Statistical assessment of distribution differences for non-Gaussian data.
Calculation of False Positive Risk (FPR) and likelihood ratio of the null hypothesis against the alternative hypothesis to calibrate p-values.
Construction using [0.05, 0.95] interquartile range for robust statistics and calibration priors.
Evaluation of Support Vector Machine (SVM) classification performance.
Selection of optimal hyperparameters (cost and gamma) for SVM models.
Bayesian Optimization selection function for hyperparameter tuning.
Visualization of changes in outline shape and calculation of central shape configurations.
Approximation of the area of oscilloscope curves to evaluate smoothness.

hypothesis_testing.test: Multivariate Analysis of Variance (MANOVA) using Hotelling–Lawley or Wilk’s Lambda test statistics; univariate Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) or Kruskal–Wallis tests; Shapiro–Wilk test for normality; Sellke–Berger approach for p-value calibration.
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There are no statistical differences or similarities among the groups (e.g., malignant vs. benign lesions, or among specific cancer types); sample distributions are homogeneous or fit a Gaussian distribution.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There are significant statistical differences or separability among the groups (e.g., malignant lesions are morphologically distinct from benign lesions); sample distributions do not fit a Gaussian distribution.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study explicitly rejected the 'blind' use of p < 0.05 for defining statistical significance. Instead, it adopted a more robust p-value threshold of 0.003 (associated with 3 standard deviations from the mean and a 4.5% chance of Type I error with 0.5 prior probabilities). Significance was interpreted using False Positive Risk (FPR) calculated via the Sellke–Berger approach with prior probabilities of 0.5 (and 0.8/0.2 where possible). Results with FPR values below the threshold or p-values below 0.003 were considered conclusive evidence for the alternative hypothesis (e.g., separation between malignant and benign lesions).

Dermofit Dataset.notes: The best-performing approach was the combination of EFA coefficients and asymmetry (Combined EFA & Asymmetry), achieving a balanced accuracy of 0.786 and an AUC of 0.735. SVMs identified benign lesions with greater accuracy than malignant lesions. Asymmetry was identified as a considerable component for identifying malignant lesions.

architecture_used.main_model: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel

input_features: Principal Component (PC) scores from Elliptic Fourier Analysis (EFA) coefficients
architecture_details.dimensionality_reduction: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) filtering PC scores representing up to 95% sample variance
architecture_details.implementation: R programming language (v.4.0.4) using the 'caret' library

preprocessing_techniques: Image segmentation using k-means clustering algorithm with 4 classes to refine manual boundaries
Morphological closing algorithm to remove isolated areas and clean segmented images
Geometric alignment and centering of lesion outlines using variance–covariance matrices and eigenvalues
Scaling of outlines using geometry centroid sizes to normalize for pixel size and camera distance
Extraction of lesion boundaries defined by 300 points
conclusions: The combination of Elliptic Fourier Analysis (EFA) coefficients and asymmetry indices is the most effective approach for distinguishing between malignant Non-Melanoma Skin Cancer (NMSC) and benign lesions, achieving a balanced accuracy of 0.786 and an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.735.

Shape irregularity and asymmetry are fundamental morphological descriptors that significantly differentiate malignant lesions (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Intraepithelial Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma) from benign lesions (Seborrhoeic Keratosis, Melanocytic Nevus).
Malignant lesions exhibit significantly higher morphological variance and asymmetry compared to benign lesions, which tend to be more circular and symmetric.

Automated segmentation using k-means clustering and morphological closing algorithms provides a reproducible and precise method for defining lesion boundaries from standard clinical images, refining manual expert segmentations.

The study supports the integration of objective shape characterization and asymmetry metrics into future clinical screening tools and Artificial Intelligence-based diagnostic methodologies for early NMSC detection.

Future work should focus on developing more efficient segmentation procedures and integrating morphological data with electromagnetic (color/texture) information to create more robust diagnostic tools.
important_remarks: The study relies on the Dermofit Dataset, which consists of standard camera images without dermoscopes or scale bars, limiting the analysis to shape rather than form (shape and size) and potentially reducing diagnostic precision compared to dermoscopic images.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, particularly between individual malignant subtypes and benign lesions (e.g., ≈97:13 ratio in the worst case), which necessitates the use of balanced accuracy and AUC metrics rather than simple accuracy.
Benign lesions were grouped into a single class (BEN) combining Seborrhoeic Keratosis and Melanocytic Nevus, preventing the model from distinguishing between different types of benign pathologies.

The study did not perform external validation; results were derived solely from the Dermofit Dataset using 10-fold cross-validation, raising concerns about generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

Segmentation was refined using k-means clustering and morphological closing on manually provided masks; while this improved boundary precision, the reliance on initial expert masks introduces potential bias if the automated refinement fails to correct poor initial segmentations.

The study explicitly rejected the standard p < 0.05 threshold in favor of p < 0.003 (associated with 3 standard deviations) to reduce Type I errors, providing a more conservative statistical interpretation.

The methodology is limited to pure shape characterization; it does not integrate color or texture features, which are critical components of standard dermatological diagnostic criteria (e.g., ABCDE mnemonic).

The study population is implied to be elderly and white-skinned (as noted in the introduction regarding NMSC incidence), which may limit the applicability of the findings to darker skin types where visual contrast and lesion appearance differ.
No dermoscopic images were used; the dataset covers only the visible area of the electromagnetic spectrum, which may hinder the detection of un-pigmented lesions or subsurface structures.
